---
title: "2.3 if Statements"
subtitle: "Part 2: if-else and Common Errors"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit2-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Two-Way Selection

### Pick between two possibilities

What if you want to pick between two possibilities?

In programming, you can use the <span class="term">if</span> keyword followed by a statement or block of statements and then the <span class="term">else</span> keyword also followed by a statement or block of statements.

## If Else Syntax

### Block and single-statement forms

```java
// A block if/else statement
if (boolean expression)
{
   statement1;
   statement2;
}
else
{
   do other statement;
   and another one;
}

// A single if/else statement
if (boolean expression)
    Do statement;
else
    Do other statement;
```

## Two-Way Selection

### Exactly one segment executes

A <span class="term">two-way selection</span> (if-else statement) is used when there are two segments of code: one to be executed when the Boolean expression is true and another segment for when the Boolean expression is false.

The body of the if is executed when the Boolean expression is true, and the body of the else is executed when the Boolean expression is false.

![The order that statements execute in an if/else statement](../../assets/2-selection-and-iteration/2-3-if-statements/Condition-two.png){fig-align="center" width="34%"}

## Code Task

### `activecode:: ifHeads`

Textbook prompt:

::: {.tight-list}
- Try the following code.
- If `isHeads` is true it will print `Let's go to the game`.
- Then it will print `after conditional`.
:::

## Code Window

### `activecode:: ifHeads`

::: {.code-scroll}
```java
public class Test2
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        boolean isHeads = true;
        if (isHeads)
        {
            System.out.println("Let's go to the game");
        }
        else
        {
            System.out.println("Let's watch a movie");
        }
        System.out.println("after conditional");
    }
}
```
:::

## Expected Output

### `ifHeads`

The Runestone test expects:

```text
Let's go to the game
after conditional
```

Changing `isHeads` to `false` makes the else branch execute before `after conditional`.

## Trace Checkpoint

### `fillintheblank:: falseElse`

Textbook prompt: Try changing the code above to `boolean isHeads = false;`. What line will be printed before the `after conditional`?

Correct response:

```text
Let's watch a movie
```

Reasoning: If the Boolean value is false, the statement following the else will execute.

## Relational If Else

### Test both parts of the code

If/else statements can also be used with relational operators and numbers.

If your code has an if/else statement, you need to test it with <span class="mark">2 test-cases</span> to make sure that both parts of the code work.

## Code Task

### `activecode:: licenseifelse`

Textbook prompt: Run the following code to see what it prints out when `age` is `16`. Change `age` to `15` and run it again to see the result of the print statement in the else part.

Can you change the if-statement to indicate that you can get a license at age `15` instead of `16`?

## Code Window

### `activecode:: licenseifelse`

::: {.code-scroll}
```java
public class DriversLicenseTest
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        int age = 16;
        if (age >= 16)
        {
            System.out.println("You can get a driver's license in most states!");
        }
        else
        {
            System.out.println(
                    "Sorry, you need to be older to get a driver's license.");
        }
    }
}
```
:::

## Test Requirements

### `activecode:: licenseifelse`

Runestone checks that the code contains:

```java
age >= 15
```

Use 2 test cases for the value of `age` to test your code and see the results of both print statements.

## Mixed-Up Code

### `parsonsprob:: ifelseevenOdd`

Textbook prompt: The following program should print out `"x is even"` if the remainder of `x` divided by `2` is `0` and `"x is odd"` otherwise, but the code is mixed up.

## Mixed Blocks

### `parsonsprob:: ifelseevenOdd`

Reorder these blocks:

```text
public class EvenOrOdd
{
public static void main(String[] args)
{
int x = 92;
if (x % 2 == 0)
{
System.out.println("x is even");
}
else
{
System.out.println("x is odd");
}
}
}
```

## Correct Order

### `parsonsprob:: ifelseevenOdd`

```java
public class EvenOrOdd
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        int x = 92;
        if (x % 2 == 0)
        {
            System.out.println("x is even");
        }
        else
        {
            System.out.println("x is odd");
        }
    }
}
```

## Code Task

### `activecode:: scoreifelse`

Textbook prompt: Try the following code. Add an else statement to the if statement that prints out `"Good job!"` if the score is greater than `9`. Change the value of `score` to test it.

Can you change the Boolean test to only print out `"Good job"` if the score is greater than `20`?

## Code Window

### `activecode:: scoreifelse`

```java
public class ScoreTest
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        int score = 8;
        if (score <= 9)
        {
            System.out.println("Try for a higher score!");
        }
    }
}
```

## Test Requirements

### `activecode:: scoreifelse`

Runestone checks that student code:

::: {.tight-list}
- changes the original code
- contains `else`
- contains either `score <= 20` or `score > 20`
- prints `Good job!` when `score` is changed to `25`
- prints `Try for a higher score!` when `score` is changed to `5`
:::

## Common Errors

### Rules to follow with if statements

::: {.tight-list}
- Always use curly braces `{` and `}` to enclose the block of statements under the if condition.
- Don't put in a semicolon `;` after the first line of the if statement, `if (test);`.
- Always use `==`, not `=`, in the condition of an if statement to test a variable.
- The `else` statement matches with the closest `if` statement.
:::

If you want to match an `else` with a different `if` statement, use curly braces to group the `if` and `else` together.

## Debug Task

### `activecode:: if-missing-curlies`

Textbook prompt: The code below doesn't work as expected. Fix it to only print `Wear a coat` and `Wear gloves` when `isCold` is true.

```java
public class Test1
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        boolean isCold = false;
        if (isCold = true);
            System.out.println("Wear a coat");
            System.out.println("Wear gloves");
    }
}
```

## Test Requirements

### `activecode:: if-missing-curlies`

Runestone expects empty output when `isCold` is false:

```text

```

It also checks that the fixed code has at least `3` opening braces `{`.

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should include

::: {.tight-list}
- describe a two-way selection as choosing exactly one of two segments
- test both true and false paths for an if/else statement
- use `% 2 == 0` to separate even and odd outcomes
- preserve starter code blanks until students complete the task
- identify missing braces, stray semicolons, and `=` versus `==`
- explain which `if` an `else` matches
:::

## End

### Continue to Part 3

[Next: Magic 8 Ball challenge →](2-3-part-3-magic-8-ball.html)
